Learning Objective 39A · Bones of the Week

Squamous Bones of the Neurocranium

Describe and demonstrate the Frontal, Parietal, Temporal, and Occipital bones; familiarize yourself with the Sphenoid.

Frontal Parietal Temporal Occipital Sphenoid

Contents

1Overview of the Neurocranium 2Frontal Bone 3Parietal Bone 4Temporal Bone 5Occipital Bone 6Sphenoid Bone 7Sutures & Craniometric Points 8Cranial Base & Foramina 9Quick-Reference Summary 10Self-Assessment Quiz 11MCQ (20 Questions)

Overview of the Neurocranium

The cranium (skull) is the skeleton of the head, composed of 22 named bones. It divides into the neurocranium (brain case) and the viscerocranium (facial skeleton).

The neurocranium houses the brain and its meninges, the proximal cranial nerves, and the cerebral vasculature. In adults it is formed by 8 bones: 4 singular midline bones (frontal, ethmoidal, sphenoidal, occipital) and 2 bilateral pairs (temporal ×2, parietal ×2).

The neurocranium has a dome-like roof — the calvaria (skullcap) — and a floor — the cranial base (basicranium). Calvarial bones are primarily flat bones formed by intramembranous ossification. Cranial base bones are irregular bones formed by endochondral ossification of cartilage (chondrocranium).

Fig 8.1A — Adult cranium, lateral view. Landmarks: temporal fossa, bregma, lambda, parietal bone, frontal bone, sphenoid, occipital bone, zygomatic arch, external acoustic meatus, mastoid and styloid processes.
Fig 8.1B,C. (B) Neurocranium (upper) vs. viscerocranium (lower) — the neurocranium volume is ~2× viscerocranium. (C) Inferior view showing sphenoid and occipital bones with the foramen magnum.

Frontal Bone

Bone of the Week

The frontal bone forms the skeleton of the forehead via its squamous (flat) part. It articulates inferiorly with the nasal and zygomatic bones. A horizontal orbital part forms the roof of the orbit and part of the anterior cranial fossa floor.

Key Landmarks

Glabella — smooth, depressed area between the superciliary arches. Superciliary arch — ridge superior to supra-orbital margin, more prominent in males. Supra-orbital foramen/notch — transmits supra-orbital nerve and vessels. Frontal eminence — smooth prominence on squamous part. Frontal crest — median internal bony extension. Frontal (metopic) suture — divides L/R frontal in neonates, persists in ~8% of adults.

The frontal bone is pneumatized (contains the frontal sinuses). It also articulates with the lacrimal, ethmoid, and sphenoid bones.

Clinical Pearl
The frontal bone forms much of the anterior cranial fossa floor. Fractures here can tear meninges and cause CSF rhinorrhea (leak through the nose via the cribriform plate region).
Fig 8.2 — Anterior (facial) aspect. Frontal bone identified with squamous and orbital parts, glabella, superciliary arch, supra-orbital foramen/notch. Also shows mandible parts.
Fig 8.3 — Bones of anterior aspect (color-coded). Green = frontal. Note how the frontal bone frames the superior orbit, contains the frontal eminence, and displays the frontal (metopic) suture remnant.

Parietal Bone

Bone of the Week

The paired parietal bones form the largest part of the calvaria (sides and roof). They are classic flat bones — convex externally, concave internally.

Key Landmarks

Parietal eminence — widest point of the skull. Superior & inferior temporal lines — arch across external surface (attachment for temporalis fascia and muscle). Parietal foramen — small, inconstant opening near the sagittal suture transmitting emissary veins connecting scalp veins to the superior sagittal sinus.

Articulations (4 Suture Borders)

Sagittal suture (with opposite parietal), coronal suture (with frontal), lambdoid suture (with occipital), squamous suture (with temporal and sphenoid).

Fig 8.8 — Superior aspect of calvaria. (A) Color-coded: frontal, parietal, occipital. Shows coronal, sagittal, and lambdoid sutures with bregma and lambda. (B) Bregma detail. (C) Lambda and parietal foramen.

Temporal Bone

Bone of the Week

The paired temporal bones contribute to the lateral cranial wall and the cranial base. Each has four parts:

Squamous part — large, flat fan-shaped portion forming the lateral skull wall; contains the mandibular fossa (TMJ articulation). Petrous part (L., rock-like) — dense pyramid at the cranial base housing the inner ear and internal acoustic meatus. Tympanic part — surrounds the external acoustic meatus opening. Mastoid part — postero-inferior, features the mastoid process (SCM attachment) and mastoid air cells.

Key Processes

Zygomatic process — joins the zygomatic bone to form the zygomatic arch. Styloid process — slender, needle-like projection anteromedial to the mastoid. External acoustic meatus — entrance to the ear canal leading to the tympanic membrane.

Clinical Pearl
Stylomastoid foramen — posterior to the styloid process base, transmits CN VII (facial nerve) and the stylomastoid artery. The jugular foramen between the occipital and petrous temporal transmits CN IX, X, XI and the internal jugular vein.
Fig 8.4A — Lateral aspect, color-coded. Blue = temporal (squamous + mastoid parts), green = frontal, orange = parietal, red = sphenoid (greater wing). Pterion at the junction of all four.
Fig 8.4B,C — Sutural (wormian) bones. (B) Along temporoparietal suture. (C) Along lambdoid suture.

Occipital Bone

Bone of the Week

The occipital bone forms the posterior cranium and much of the cranial base. It articulates with the sphenoid anteriorly. Its four parts are arranged around the foramen magnum:

Squamous part — large posterior plate with the external occipital protuberance (palpable landmark = inion) and the superior & inferior nuchal lines. Basilar part (clivus) — anterior to foramen magnum, fuses with sphenoid. Lateral parts (×2) — bear the occipital condyles for articulation with C1 (atlas), allowing nodding.

Foramen Magnum — Major Contents

Spinal cord (becomes medulla oblongata), meninges, vertebral arteries, anterior & posterior spinal arteries, spinal accessory nerve (CN XI).

Fig 8.7 — Occipital (posterior) aspect. (A) The occiput showing parts of parietal bones, occipital bone, and mastoid temporal bones. Sagittal and lambdoid sutures meet at lambda. (B) Posterior cranial fossa with squamous occipital removed.

Sphenoid Bone

Familiarize

The sphenoid (L., winged) is an unpaired, irregular, pneumatized bone wedged between the frontal, temporal, and occipital bones. It consists of a body + 3 pairs of processes:

Greater wings — spread laterally; have orbital, temporal, infratemporal, and cerebral surfaces. Lesser wings — smaller, superior; form part of the anterior cranial fossa floor. Pterygoid processes — extend inferiorly with lateral and medial pterygoid plates.

The body contains the sella turcica (Turkish saddle) with the hypophysial fossa housing the pituitary gland. Bounded posteriorly by the dorsum sellae and posterior clinoid processes, anteriorly by the anterior clinoid processes of the lesser wings.

Fig 8.10 — Sphenoid bone. (A) Anterior view. (B) Posterior view. (C) Superior view showing sella turcica, optic canals, and relationships of greater/lesser wings and pterygoid processes. Complete abbreviation key at right.

Sutures & Craniometric Points

Most calvarial bones are united by fibrous interlocking sutures. During childhood, some bones (sphenoid and occipital) are united by hyaline cartilage (synchondroses).

Major Sutures

SutureBones Separated
CoronalFrontal from Parietal
SagittalRight Parietal from Left Parietal
LambdoidParietal & Temporal from Occipital
SquamousTemporal (squamous) and Parietal
Frontal (Metopic)R & L Frontal (neonatal; ~8% persist)

Craniometric Points (Table 8.1)

LandmarkLocation
PterionJunction of sphenoid (greater wing), squamous temporal, frontal, and parietal. Overlies middle meningeal artery.
BregmaJunction of coronal + sagittal sutures
LambdaJunction of lambdoid + sagittal sutures
VertexSuperior-most point of neurocranium
AsterionJunction of 3 sutures: parietomastoid, occipitomastoid, lambdoid
GlabellaSmooth prominence on frontal bone superior to nose root
InionMost prominent point of external occipital protuberance
NasionJunction of frontonasal and internasal sutures
Clinical Pearl — Pterion
The pterion is the thinnest part of the calvaria. Trauma here can rupture the middle meningeal artery, causing an epidural hematoma — a neurosurgical emergency.
Fig 8.6 — Craniometric points, lateral view. Shows vertex, bregma, pterion, lambda, glabella, nasion, inion, and asterion on the skull.

Cranial Base & Foramina

The cranial base (basicranium) is the inferior portion of the neurocranium. Internally, it has three stepped depressions forming the bowl-shaped floor: the anterior (highest), middle, and posterior (deepest) cranial fossae.

Fig 8.9A — External cranial base (color-coded). Contributing bones: frontal, maxilla, parietal, sphenoid, temporal, occipital, palatine, vomer, zygomatic.
Fig 8.9B — External cranial base features. Key structures: foramen magnum, occipital condyles, jugular foramen, carotid canal, foramen ovale, foramen spinosum, mandibular fossa, stylomastoid foramen.
Fig 8.11 — Cranial foramina. Superior (internal) and inferior (external) views with color-coded bones and all major foramina labeled.

Quick-Reference Summary

Frontal Bone

  • Squamous part = forehead
  • Orbital part = roof of orbit / anterior fossa floor
  • Glabella, superciliary arch, supra-orbital foramen/notch, frontal eminence, frontal crest
  • Pneumatized (frontal sinuses)
  • Ossification: intramembranous

Parietal Bone (×2)

  • Largest contributor to calvaria
  • Classic flat bone (convex ext / concave int)
  • Parietal eminence, sup & inf temporal lines, parietal foramen
  • 4 suture borders: coronal, sagittal, lambdoid, squamous
  • Ossification: intramembranous

Temporal Bone (×2)

  • 4 parts: squamous, petrous, tympanic, mastoid
  • Squamous → mandibular fossa (TMJ)
  • Petrous → inner ear, internal acoustic meatus
  • Processes: zygomatic, styloid, mastoid
  • Pneumatized (mastoid air cells)
  • Ossification: mixed

Occipital Bone

  • 4 parts: squamous, basilar, 2× lateral
  • Foramen magnum → cord, vertebral aa., CN XI
  • Occipital condyles → atlas (C1)
  • Ext. occipital protuberance (inion), sup/inf nuchal lines
  • Ossification: mixed

Self-Assessment Quiz

Click to reveal answers

1How many bones form the adult neurocranium? Name them.

8 bones: 4 singular (frontal, ethmoid, sphenoid, occipital) + 2 bilateral pairs (temporal ×2, parietal ×2).

2What is the calvaria, and which bones primarily form it?

The calvaria (skullcap) is the dome-like roof of the neurocranium, formed primarily by the squamous parts of the frontal and occipital bones and the paired parietal bones.

3What is the pterion? Why is it clinically significant?

H-shaped junction of sphenoid (greater wing), squamous temporal, frontal, and parietal. Thinnest calvaria → overlies the middle meningeal artery → trauma causes epidural hematoma.

4Name the four parts of the temporal bone.

Squamous (lateral wall, mandibular fossa), Petrous (cranial base, inner ear), Tympanic (surrounds ext. acoustic meatus), Mastoid (mastoid process, air cells).

5What structures pass through the foramen magnum?

Spinal cord (→ medulla), meninges, vertebral arteries, anterior & posterior spinal arteries, spinal accessory nerve (CN XI).

6What type of ossification forms calvaria flat bones vs. cranial base bones?

Calvaria: intramembranous (from neural crest mesenchyme). Cranial base: primarily endochondral (from cartilage/chondrocranium).

7Which suture separates frontal from parietal? Both parietals from each other?

Coronal suture (frontal ↔ parietal). Sagittal suture (R parietal ↔ L parietal).

8Identify: junction of sagittal + lambdoid sutures.

Lambda — resembles the Greek letter Λ.

9The sphenoid body houses what fossa, and what structure sits in it?

The sella turcica contains the hypophysial fossa → houses the pituitary gland.

10Occipital condyles articulate with which vertebra? What movement?

Atlas (C1) at the atlanto-occipital joint → primarily flexion/extension (nodding "yes").
